--- SOURCE 1: Evidence Brief | What is Technology's Impact on Social Health? ---
URL: https://www.socialconnectionguidelines.org/en/evidence-briefs/what-is-technology-s-impact-on-social-health

SUMMARY:
With respect to the social benefits of technology use, researchers have hypothesized that social media and communication technologies can facilitate social interactions and support – providing opportunities to meet new people and stay connected (Winstone et al., 2021; Nick et al., 2018). Despite evidence for displacement, studies have generally found that internet use does not have a particularly strong influence on levels of social interaction at the individual level (Hall et al., 2017). Even when people are not using technologies to displace in-person social connection or engage in cognitive escape, studies indicate that even the mere presence of a smartphone during a social interaction can deteriorate the quality of the interaction (Dwyer et al.; 2017; Ward et al., 2017; McDaniel & Coyne et al., 2016; Katz & Aakhus et al., 2002) – though here too study findings are mixed (Sprecher et al., 2016).

Evidence brief

What is technology’s impact on social health?
=============================================

January 31, 2024

Simran Lail, Adam Frost, Jocelle Refol, Kiffer Card

### Background

With the emergence of new technologies, including smartphones and social media platforms, it has never been easier to reach out to others, connect at a moment’s notice, or keep in touch over long distances (Plotkin, [2012](https://www.amazon.ca/Communication-Cyberspace-Robert-Plotkin/dp/0816077533)). Yet, while access to these tools has increased over the last several decades (Spiegelman & Detsky, [2008](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/article-abstract/181588)), some studies suggest that we are becoming less socially connected. In fact, evidence has emerged that we have fewer strong social ties (Cox, [2021](https://www.americansurveycenter.org/research/the-state-of-american-friendship-change-challenges-and-loss/); Twenge et al., [2019](https://doi.org/10.1177/0265407519836170); Brashears & Brashears, [2015](https://doi.org/10.1002/9781118900772.etrds0085); Brashears, [2011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socnet.2011.10.003); McPherson et al., [2006](https://doi.org/10.1177/000312240607100301)) and spend less of our time socializing than we did in decades past (Kannan & Veazie, [2023](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.ssmph.2022.101331)). Reinforcing worries about the harmful health effects of digital technologies, rates of poor mental health (particularly in young people) have increased along with the ubiquity of digital tools, such as the internet, social media, and smartphones (Dykxhoorn et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291723002349); Bhattarai et al., [2020](https://doi.org/10.1017%2FS2045796020000281); Goodwin et al., [2020](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.jpsychires.2020.08.014); Marquez & Long, [2020](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12187-020-09788-8); Twenge et al., [2019](https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1037/abn0000410); Knowalek & Wolanczyk, [2018](https://doi.org/10.1007%2Fs10578-018-0791-y); Bor et al., [2014](https://doi.org/10.1177/0004867414533834)). Of course, an association through time between the emergence of digital technologies and worsening mental health does not necessarily imply that one causes the other (Twenge et al., [2010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpr.2009.10.005); Maughan et al., [2005](https://doi.org/10.1097/01.yco.0000172055.25284.f2); Smith & Rutter, [1995](https://www.research.ed.ac.uk/en/publications/psychosocial-disorders-in-young-people-time-trends-and-their-caus)). Nevertheless, given the potential for harm and the widespread uptake of digital technologies, it is important for us to understand how these tools impact our wellbeing.

### Purpose

The purpose of this evidence brief is to review the impact of digital technologies on our mental and social health. In doing so, we primarily focus on internet-enabled and communication technologies, though recognize that other technological devices and platforms, such as television and video games, also impact our wellbeing.

### Evidence from Existing Studies

#### **_Uptake and Use of Digital Technologies_**

Digital technologies are now commonplace and we are exposed to these tools at earlier ages than ever before. The introduction of these technologies has taken place without systematic evidence ensuring their safety (Courage & Troseth, [2016](https://www.child-encyclopedia.com/technology-early-childhood-education/according-experts/infants-toddlers-and-learning-screen-media); Hirsh-Pasek et al., [2015](https://doi.org/10.1177/1529100615569721); Meyer et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1080/17482798.2021.1882516)). In fact, on average young children now spend several hours a day using digital technologies – representing a considerable shift in processes related to human development (Meyer et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1080/17482798.2021.1882516)). Patterns and types of use at early ages are diverse, and include using apps to learn, develop skills and find entertaining content (Meyer et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1080/17482798.2021.1882516); Hirsh-Pasek et al., [2015](https://doi.org/10.1177/1529100615569721); Meyer et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1080/17482798.2021.1882516); Ponti et al., [2017](https://doi.org/10.1093/pch/pxx123)). As children age, they branch out and begin using apps, websites, and other communication tools to connect with friends and family (Winstone et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12889-021-11802-9); U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html); Khalaf et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.7759/cureus.42990); Mollborn et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.alcr.2020.100379); Villanti et al., [2017](https://doi.org/10.2196/jmir.7303)). According to available estimates, up to 95% of youth in the United States are using social media to connect with their peers – and similar levels of use are likely in other countries (U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html)) and also among adults more generally (Crouch & Gordon, [2019](https://doi.org/10.2196/11451); Hamid et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.18502/ijph.v52i2.11877); Vătămănescu et al., [2016](https://lumenpublishing.com/journals/index.php/brain/index)). Even older adults who grew up in an era with fewer digital technologies are beginning to adopt these tools at higher and higher rates. As the social networks of older adults shrink (Fingerman et al., [2020](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32997465/)) many of them use technology to ward off feelings of loneliness and navigate their social lives (Chopik, [2016](https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2016.0151); Fingerman et al., [2020](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32997465/); Mitzner et al., [2010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2010.06.020)). Of course, uptake of technology is patchwork, being more common among those with higher incomes and education (Crouch & Gordon, [2019](https://doi.org/10.2196/11451); Fingerman et al., [2020](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32997465/)). People use different technologies with varying levels of intensity to meet different needs, and with varying levels of success (Ruggiero, [2000](https://doi.org/10.1207/S15327825MCS0301_02)). Despite these variations in use, it is clear that technology now plays a very important part in our individual and collective social lives.

#### **_The Social and Health Impacts of Technology Use_**

Given the pervasiveness of digital technologies throughout our lives, many studies have investigated the effects these have on our wellbeing. Taken together, these studies have produced mixed effects (U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html); Yavich et al., [2019](http://dx.doi.org/10.5539/hes.v9n2p10); Escobar-Viera et al., [2018](https://doi.org/10.2196/10496)) – with the balance of evidence suggesting a small to moderate influence on individual wellbeing (Huang et al., [2010](https://doi.org/10.1089/cyber.2009.0217)). However, even these results must be interpreted with caution. Most studies lack clearly defined theoretical frameworks and fail to describe the specific pathways that might account for observed effects (Bekalu et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.2196/43191)). As well, there are considerable variations in which technologies are studied and how technology use is measured (Escobar-Viera et al., [2018](https://doi.org/10.2196/10496)). Given these mixed results and the varying quality of contributing studies, it is necessary to consider both the positive and negative effects to get a full picture of how technology influences our social health.

#### **_Social Benefits of Technology_**

Individuals report a wide variety of benefits from digital technologies (Sun, [2023](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40359-023-01398-7); Dietz-Uhler & Bishop-Clark, [2001](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0747-5632(01)00006-1)). For example, it is evident that they have considerable potential to make some aspects of life easier and more enjoyable (Hirsh-Pasek et al., [2015](https://doi.org/10.1177/1529100615569721); Ponti et al., [2017](https://doi.org/10.1093/pch/pxx123); U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html)). With respect to the social benefits of technology use, researchers have hypothesized that social media and communication technologies can facilitate social interactions and support – providing opportunities to meet new people and stay connected (Winstone et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12889-021-11802-9); Nick et al., [2018](https://doi.org/10.1037/pas0000558)). Exemplifying this, Cole et al., ([2017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2016.11.058)) showed that young people with weaker in-person social support are able to strategically leverage social media to get the support they otherwise lacked. Some studies even suggest that technology may facilitate greater levels of social interaction and social support. For example, Wang & Wellman ([2010](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0002764209356247)) reported that heavy internet users actually had the most friends of any group. However, researchers also note that the forms of social connection facilitated online appear to preference weak ties, rather than strong ones (Patulny & Seaman, [2017](https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1177/1440783316674358); Twenge et al., [2013](https://doi.org/10.1515/jcc-2013-0003); Robinson & Martin; [2010](https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1177/0894439309335230); Hampton et al., [2009](https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2009/11/04/social-isolation-and-new-technology/); Shklovski et al., [2006](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2007-08867-017)) – perhaps leading to lesser social embeddedness overall despite an increase in diverse social interactions.

However, while it’s unclear whether these platforms actually help individuals form meaningful relationships with a greater number of people (Dunbar, [2016](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rsos.150292)), it is clear that people use them to connect and that such connection is beneficial (Hall et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10902-022-00581-8); Burholt et al., [2020](https://doi.org/10.1093/geront/gnaa040); Chopik, [2016](https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2016.0151); Best et al., [2015](https://doi.org/10.1108/JPMH-05-2014-0022); Mitzner et al., [2010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2010.06.020); Baym et al., [2004](https://doi.org/10.1177/1461444804041438)). One way these platforms assist with this is by helping people find communities who share their interests or life experiences (Arky, [2023](https://childmind.org/article/how-parent-support-groups-can-help/); Hamid et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.18502/ijph.v52i2.11877); Khalaf et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.7759/cureus.42990); U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html); Woo & Chase, [2022](https://doi.org/10.21896/jksmch.2022.26.3.183); Dienlin & Johannes, [2020](https://doi.org/10.31887/dcns.2020.22.2/tdienlin); Crouch & Gordon, [2019](https://doi.org/10.2196/11451); DeHoff et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.2196/jmir.6722); Vătămănescu et al., [2016](https://lumenpublishing.com/journals/index.php/brain/index)). For isolated individuals, such as older adults, the internet can help them overcome physical and geographic barriers that contribute to isolation (Petersen et al., [2023](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37034933/); Kusumota et al., [2022](https://doi.org/10.1590/1518-8345.5641.3573); Chopik et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.1089/cyber.2016.0151)). In these instances, technology use can be lifeline.

More generally, people use technology to stay in touch with each other. In fact, to some extent, it may even be necessary to use digital forms of social connection in order to maintain relationships and connections with other people (Venter et al., [2019](https://www.scielo.org.za/scielo.php?script=sci_arttext&pid=S0259-94222019000100020); Turkle et al., [2012](https://www.amazon.ca/Alone-Together-Expect-Technology-Other/dp/0465031463)). Underscoring this reality, one experimental study found that participants asked to abstain from social media use actually experienced lower wellbeing as a result (Vally & D’Souza, [2019](https://doi.org/10.1111/ppc.12431)) – likely because abstaining from social media leads to isolation as individuals are cut off from their friends and family. In sum, it is clear that technology is widely used to facilitate social connections and maintain relationships and that such use of technology has many benefits (Patulny & Seaman, [2017](https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1177/1440783316674358)).

#### **_Harms of Technology_**

Without discounting the potential benefits and social uses of technology, research also suggest that digital technologies can cause harm. For example, researchers have sought to understand whether computer-mediated social interaction _replaces_ in-person, face-to-face social activities. For example, Rosenfeld et al. ([2019](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1908630116)) found that online dating platforms began displacing other ways of meeting romantic partners in the mid-1990s. Despite evidence for displacement, studies have generally found that internet use does not have a particularly strong influence on levels of social interaction at the individual level (Hall et al., [2017](https://doi.org/10.1080/1369118X.2018.1430162)). While researchers have documented declines in face-to-face contact that coincide with rises in technology-mediated contact, social technologies are generally associated with greater, not less perceived social support (Patulny & Seaman, [2017](https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1177/1440783316674358)). Importantly, however, even if at the individual-level technology use is not associated with being less socially connected, at a group level the pervasive presence of technology could make social connections more difficult. This viewpoint is supported by Twenge et al. ([2019](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0265407519836170)), who noted that technological displacement of social interactions occurs at the cohort level across generations, but not at the individual level. Providing additional support for this, Kushlev et al., ([2017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2017.07.001)) conducted an intriguing study by asking participants to find an unfamiliar building and participate in a problem-solving task. Participants were randomly assigned to either use their smart phone or not. Those who used their smartphones, experienced benefits to their mood (because the task was easier) but overall felt less connected. This study highlights the ways in which relying on technology displaces our reliance on others – leading to potentially harmful outcomes (Rushkoff, [2021](http://dx.doi.org/10.2478/nimmir-2021-0007); Turkle, [2012](https://www.amazon.ca/Alone-Together-Expect-Technology-Other/dp/0465031463); Pickett, [2004](https://doi.org/10.1108/00197850410556694)). Thus, while difficult to detect – due to the positive associations between technology use and social connectedness on an individual-level – technology does appear to displace social connections at least to some extent.

To understand whether the displacement of face-to-face social connections is a problem, researchers have sought to compare in-person face-to-face social interactions with computer-mediated ones. While it is impossible to say that face-to-face connections are universally beneficial in all situations and for all peoples (Rhoads, [2010](https://doi.org/10.1177/0885412210382984)), the existing literature does support the idea that face-to-face interactions are generally superior to computer-mediated ones across a wide variety of measures (Dhakal et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1093/geronb/gbac136); Hall et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1093/jcmc/zmac026); Stieger et al., [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-34957-4); Gruber et al., [2022](https://www.hope.uzh.ch/scoms/article/view/3340); Hadley et al., [2022](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44159-021-00008-w); Marinucci et al., [2022](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2021.107102); Ransom et al., [2022](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8880930/); Behrens et al., [2019](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10919-019-00314-1); Simone et al., [2019](https://doi.org/10.1007%2Fs11136-019-02232-7); Sacco & Ismail et al., [2014](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2014.04.004); Okdie et al., [2011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2010.07.017); Lee et al., [2010](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11205-010-9618-3)). For example, Petrova & Schulz et al., ([2022](https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2022.2043244)) found that following engagement in less life-like social interactions, individuals report feeling more lonely, sadder, less affectionate, less happy, and less supported. Explaining the superiority of in-person social connection, studies have shown that they better facilitate non-verbal communications (which make up a substantial component of human social behaviour; Beyan et al., [2022](https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3626516); Eddy, [2019](https://doi.org/10.1145/3340764.3344451)) and self-disclosure (which is key to the development of intimacy; Ruppel et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.1111/jcc4.12179)). As well, emerging evidence indicates that physical touch and presence are fundamentally beneficial to individuals (Packheiser et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.06.20.23291651); Taneja et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-019-01253-8); Field, [2010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dr.2011.01.001)). These and other advantages of in-person connection are important to consider when determining the format of social interactions.

Importantly, however, computer-mediated interactions are better than having no interactions at all (Kroencke et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1037/pspp0000422)) and people with better mental health are seen to engage in a greater variety of social contexts (both online and in-person; Simone et al., [2019](https://doi.org/10.1007%2Fs11136-019-02232-7)). This logic has led to researchers examining whether digital mediated social interactions can be used to support people who are socially isolated – though the results of studies evaluating these interventions have shown mixed results and the positive effects tend to be small (Doring et al., [2022](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph191811310); Macdonald et al., [2021](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34264899/); Noone et al., [2020](https://doi.org/10.1002/14651858.cd013632)).

In addition to displacing social connections with inferior digitally-mediated ones, new technologies can also cause direct harms. For example, the internet is full of harmful content, including misinformation, divisive posts, scams, and communications from mean-spirited individuals (Diomidous et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.5455/aim.2016.24.66-68); Leist et al., [2013](https://doi.org/10.1159/000346818)). As well, even in the absence of negative stimuli, the carefully curated nature of content online can lead to unfavorable social comparisons, which have been linked to worse body image, poorer self-esteem, and greater levels of loneliness (Gregory et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12888-023-04849-y); McComb et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1080/15213269.2023.2180647); Dibb & Foster, [2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2021.e05999)). Similarly, the abundant entertainment online can serve as attractive escaps – leading to engagement in less effective forms of coping that can lead to the deterioration of relationships and trap people in isolation (Hoge et al., [2017](https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2016-1758g)) – lowering the quality of our social interactions and our attention to others (Rotondi et al.; [2017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2017.09.001)). Even when people are not using technologies to displace in-person social connection or engage in cognitive escape, studies indicate that even the mere presence of a smartphone during a social interaction can deteriorate the quality of the interaction (Dwyer et al.; [2017](https://open.library.ubc.ca/soa/cIRcle/collections/ubctheses/24/items/1.0354413); Ward et al., [2017](https://doi.org/10.1086/691462); McDaniel & Coyne et al., [2016](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2014-52280-001); Katz & Aakhus et al., [2002](https://www.amazon.ca/Perpetual-Contact-Communication-Private-Performance/dp/0521002664)) – though here too study findings are mixed (Sprecher et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2016.03.090)). Compounding all of these problems, technology use is often sedentary – leading to poorer physical and mental health, which can further deteriorate our ability to meaningfully connect with others (Diomidous et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.5455/aim.2016.24.66-68); Harvard Health, [2018](https://www.health.harvard.edu/staying-healthy/the-surprising-side-effects-from-using-technology); Johnson, [2023](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/negative-effects-of-technology)).

#### **_Differentiating Between Harmful and Beneficial Technology Use_**

Recognizing that technology use is associated with both harms and benefits, researchers have begun to distinguish between healthy and unhealthy patterns of use (U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html); Lippke et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.2147/prbm.s274502); European Parliament, [2020](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/IDAN/2020/641540/EPRS_IDA(2020)641540_EN.pdf); Gao et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.2197); Diomidous et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.5455/aim.2016.24.66-68)). Indeed,it is increasingly recognized that not all forms of digital technology use are harmful (Seabrook et al., [2016](https://doi.org/10.2196/mental.5842)). Supporting this assertion, meta-analyses suggest that “active” forms of social engagement, including one-to-one communication, self-disclosing mutual online friendships, and uplifting online social engagements, are associated with lesser loneliness and stress (Marciano et al., [2022](https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2021.793868)). Meanwhile, “passive” (e.g., content consumption) forms of technology use appear to be less beneficial and more harmful (Ozimek et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.teler.2023.100048); Keum et al., [2022](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-022-03473-5); Valkenburg et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1177/14614448211065425); Whitley, [2020](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/talking-about-men/202002/social-media-and-mental-health-time-digital-detox)). Of course, these relationships are nuanced and ongoing research continues to highlight important mediators and moderators. For example, Bonsaksen et al., ([2023](https://doi.org/10.1080/21642850.2022.2158089)) demonstrates that greater time on social media is associated with greater loneliness – but primarily for those who use social media for social purposes. This suggests that for some individuals, striving to use social media in an “active” style may backfire. Highlighting this, it’s also important to recognize that individuals are not entirely in control of how they interface with technologies. For example, Gao et al. ([2016](https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.2197)) showed that social anxiety and loneliness was more strongly associated with the distribution of incoming calls than it was with outgoing calls. In other words, lonely individuals did not differ with respect to how many calls or texts they sent, but received fewer in-coming calls. This highlights how the broader social context in which individuals exist plays an important role in shaping their experience using internet and communication technologies.

Given these dichotomies, it is clear that technology use does not necessarily result in harms to users. However, in some cases, harmful patterns of use can cross into addiction or dependence (American Psychological Association, [2023](https://www.apa.org/topics/social-media-internet/health-advisory-adolescent-social-media-use); Dienlin & Johannes, [2020](https://doi.org/10.31887/dcns.2020.22.2/tdienlin); Garrido et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2021.e07037); Tang et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpr.2021.102021); U.S. Dept Health and Human Services, [2022](https://newsinhealth.nih.gov/2022/09/healthy-social-media-habits); U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html)) – and in such cases the harmful associations with adverse health and social outcomes have been readily demonstrated (Cai et al., [2023](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36744121/); Ge et al., [2023](https://doi.org/10.1177/00332941231180119); Huang, [2022](https://doi.org/10.1177/0020764020978434); Masaeli & Billieux, [2022](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40429-022-00415-w); Saadati et al., [2021](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34754896/); Meshi et al., [2020](https://doi.org/10.1159/000502577); Weinstein & Lejoyeux, [2010](https://doi.org/10.3109/00952990.2010.491880)). Importantly, however, the relationship between technology use and loneliness is observed to be bidirectional (Zhang et al., [2024](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addbeh.2023.107916); Pera, [2020](https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyt.2020.573473)) – suggesting that technology use not only contributes to higher loneliness, but that lonely people engage in more harmful patterns of technology use as a result of their loneliness (Nowland et al., [2018](https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691617713052)).

Of course, vulnerability to internet addiction depends on a wide variety of individual-level and social factors – but is also the product of certain elements that the majority of users are exposed to, include the contestant pressure to be accessible online, the attractive allure of complex stimuli, and other compelling elements (Kraut et al., [1998](https://doi.org/10.1037/0003-066x.53.9.1017); U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html); Winstone et al., [2021](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12889-021-11802-9)).

In order to prevent harms arising from technology use, it is recommended individuals monitor their screen time, thoughtfully consider the impact of technology on their wellbeing, and set appropriate restrictions around use (U.S. Surgeon General, [2023](https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/priorities/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html); U.S. Dept Health and Human Services, [2022](https://newsinhealth.nih.gov/2022/09/healthy-social-media-habits); CMHA BC, [2019](https://www.heretohelp.bc.ca/wellness-module/wellness-module-10-staying-mentally-healthy-with-technology); APA, [2017](https://www.apa.org/topics/social-media-internet/healthy-technology-use); Rosen et al., [2014](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2014.01.036)). In addition to setting restrictions, individuals should strive for healthy forms of engagement, including those that expose individuals to positive messaging, engender positive feelings, and help individuals engage in positive activities (Villamil & Heshmati, [2023](https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1175740)).

### Analyses from the Canadian Social Connection Survey

Using data from the 2023 Canadian Social Connection Survey we asked participants to report on their relationships with 10 other people and examined whether increasing frequency of online and in-person communication were associated with levels of relationship closeness (Scored 0 to 10). Participants reported on an average of 7.6 relationships. Linear mixed effects regression results showed that higher relationship closeness was associated with longer duration relationships (in years), greater frequency of face-to-face interactions, and greater frequency of online interactions. Notably, in the model, the effect of online interaction was stronger than that of face-to-face interaction – underscoring that close relationships are characterized by a high frequency of online interaction.

# How Does Technology Affect Our Sense of Community?

![Two smartphone-like devices are positioned closely together on a flat surface. The device on the left has a dark, reflective screen and rose gold metallic edges](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/smartphone-like-devices-with-metallic-edges-covered-in-light-fibrous-shavings.jpg)
![An overhead view captures an abstract arrangement of dried light brown palm fronds and light gray ceramic cylindrical vessels. The fronds radiate outwards from a central point, interweaving with the smooth, matte-finished ceramic forms](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/organic-modern-radial-sculpture-of-dried-palm-fronds-and-light-ceramic-vessels.jpg)

## Fundamentals

The relationship between technology and our sense of community is a complex one. At its simplest, technology offers us tools to connect with others across geographical boundaries. [Social media Meaning → Social media → Digital platforms connecting individuals, shaping perceptions, and influencing sustainable living in a complex, multi-dimensional way.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/social-media/)  platforms, messaging apps, and video conferencing allow us to maintain relationships with friends and family who live far away, and to connect with new people who share our interests.

However, this ease of connection also presents challenges. **Digital interactions can sometimes feel less meaningful than face-to-face conversations**. The curated nature of online profiles can lead to unrealistic expectations and feelings of inadequacy. Furthermore, excessive use of technology can isolate individuals, drawing them away from real-world interactions and activities.

Consider the neighborhood barbecue, once a staple of community life. Now, it might be competing for attention with streaming services and online gaming. While technology doesn’t inherently destroy community, it changes the way we experience it. It offers new ways to connect, but also demands a conscious effort to maintain a balance between our digital and physical lives.

It’s also important to recognize the digital divide. Not everyone has equal access to technology or the skills to use it effectively. This can lead to a sense of exclusion for those who are less digitally connected, exacerbating existing social inequalities.

For many, the convenience and efficiency of online communication are invaluable. Think of online support groups for individuals with rare diseases or virtual classrooms that provide educational opportunities to students in remote areas. These technologies create communities where they might not otherwise exist.

Ultimately, the impact of technology on our sense of community depends on how we choose to use it. It is a powerful tool that can either strengthen or weaken our connections with others. By being mindful of our technology use and prioritizing real-world interactions, we can harness its benefits while mitigating its potential downsides.

![A single, dried, spherical flower head, predominantly pale pink in color, is positioned slightly off-center on a muted, solid grey background. The flower exhibits numerous small, tightly packed florets, giving it a textured, spiky appearance](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/macro-view-of-a-dried-pink-spherical-flower-against-a-muted-grey-background.jpg)

### The Double-Edged Sword of Online Groups

Online groups and forums are great examples of technology’s dual effect. They enable people to connect with like-minded individuals, regardless of location. A stamp collecting group, a support forum for new parents, or a fan community for a niche TV show can provide a sense of belonging and shared identity.

On the other hand, these online communities can sometimes become echo chambers, reinforcing existing beliefs and limiting exposure to diverse perspectives. The anonymity afforded by the internet can also contribute to negative behaviors such as cyberbullying and online harassment.

![A wood branch, appearing weathered and textured, is diagonally positioned across the frame. It is intricately wrapped with light-colored, dried fibrous material, which forms wavy patterns along its length](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/water-droplets-on-fibrous-wrapped-wood-branch-with-fresh-green-sprig.jpg)

### Finding Balance in a Digital World

Cultivating a strong sense of community in the [digital age Meaning → The Digital Age is a global transformation profoundly altering human life and sustainability through technology, culture, and resource use.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/digital-age/)  requires a proactive approach. It means being intentional about creating opportunities for face-to-face interactions, limiting screen time, and engaging in activities that foster a sense of belonging. This might involve volunteering in your local community, joining a sports team, or simply making an effort to connect with your neighbors.

It also means being mindful of the way we interact with others online. Practicing empathy, avoiding online arguments, and promoting respectful dialogue can help to create a more positive and inclusive online environment.

![A modular arrangement of rectangular blocks in varying shades of reddish-brown, dark gray, and light gray wood-like material creates a stepped surface. Delicate, three-dimensional elements are placed on the top](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/modular-wood-blocks-with-miniature-botanical-elements-in-an-abstract-desktop-display.jpg)
![A macro photograph captures an abstract composition of liquid ink spreading across a light, neutral-toned surface. Dark brown and translucent grey ink forms organic, swirling patterns with soft edges and visible air bubbles](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/abstract-fluid-ink-art-organic-flow-dropper-neutral-tones-macro-photography.jpg)

## Intermediate

Beyond the basic understanding of technology’s impact on community, lies a more intricate web of social and psychological effects. The very definition of “community” is being reshaped by digital interactions, leading to both exciting possibilities and potential pitfalls. While technology can facilitate connections across vast distances, it simultaneously challenges traditional notions of local, geographically-bound communities.

One critical aspect to consider is the **shift from place-based to interest-based communities**. Historically, our sense of belonging was often tied to our physical location – our neighborhood, town, or city. Today, technology allows us to connect with people who share our passions and interests, regardless of where they live. This can be particularly valuable for individuals who feel marginalized or isolated in their local communities.

However, the ease with which we can curate our online experiences also carries risks. The algorithms that [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  [social media platforms Social media amplifies both sustainable actions and unsustainable trends, demanding critical engagement and mindful consumption.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/question/what-role-do-social-media-platforms-play/)  often prioritize content that aligns with our existing beliefs, creating filter bubbles that limit our exposure to diverse perspectives. This can lead to increased polarization and a decline in our ability to engage in constructive dialogue with those who hold different viewpoints.

The rise of remote work, accelerated by technological advancements, further complicates the relationship between technology and community. While remote work offers greater flexibility and autonomy, it can also lead to feelings of isolation and detachment from colleagues and the workplace community.

![Two human hands are shown from the wrists up, carefully arranging a stack of four smooth, rounded natural pebbles against a plain, light grey background. The stack consists of a wider, patterned grey and white stone at the bottom, followed by two solid grey stones, and topped with a textured, lighter grey stone](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/adult-hands-arranging-smooth-grey-and-white-natural-stones-into-a-balanced-stack.jpg)

### The Psychology of Online Connection

Understanding the psychological underpinnings of online interaction is essential for navigating the complexities of community in the digital age. Social comparison theory, for example, suggests that we constantly evaluate ourselves by comparing ourselves to others. Social media platforms, with their curated displays of success and happiness, can exacerbate this tendency, leading to feelings of inadequacy and low self-esteem.

Furthermore, the lack of nonverbal cues in online communication can lead to misunderstandings and misinterpretations. Facial expressions, tone of voice, and body language provide crucial context in face-to-face interactions. Their absence online can increase the likelihood of conflict and damage relationships.

Consider the following table illustrating the difference between face-to-face and online communication:

| Feature | Face-to-Face Communication | Online Communication |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Nonverbal Cues | Present | Limited or Absent |
| Context | Rich | Potentially Lacking |
| Spontaneity | High | Variable (can be asynchronous) |
| Potential for Misunderstanding | Lower | Higher |

The data highlights the inherent communication challenges within online environments.

![A cluster of abstract, textured organic forms on thin, light-colored stems is presented against a dark, desaturated background. Several forms are soft pink, covered in small, clear, bead-like textures](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/textured-pink-and-white-organic-sculptural-forms-on-delicate-stems.jpg)

### Reclaiming Community in the Digital Age

Building meaningful connections in the digital age requires a conscious effort to cultivate both online and offline relationships. This includes being mindful of our social media use, seeking out diverse perspectives, and prioritizing face-to-face interactions whenever possible.

Here are some practical strategies for strengthening your sense of community in a technology-driven world:

![A dried thistle seed head rests centrally on a textured, rough grey surface. The seed head is light brown with a pinkish hue, featuring numerous fine, spiky filaments radiating outwards, giving it a fluffy appearance](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/muted-dried-thistle-seed-head-on-textured-grey-concrete-surface.jpg)

### The Role of Technology in Sustainable Communities

Technology can also play a positive role in building sustainable communities. Smart city initiatives, for example, use technology to improve [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  efficiency, reduce waste, and enhance transportation systems. Online platforms can connect residents with local resources and opportunities, fostering a sense of civic engagement.

However, it’s crucial to ensure that these technological advancements are implemented equitably and inclusively. Otherwise, they risk [exacerbating existing social inequalities AI companions risk deepening social divides through unequal access, biased algorithms, and reinforcement of societal stereotypes.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/question/can-ai-companions-exacerbate-existing-social-inequalities/)  and creating new forms of digital exclusion.

![A textured spherical object, light peach and white with dark speckles, is shown melting onto a dark integrated circuit chip. The viscous, white substance drips and pools around the base of the sphere](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/textured-spherical-object-melting-onto-a-dark-integrated-circuit-chip.jpg)
![A carved wooden object rests on a textured wooden surface. The light-toned wood features a visible grain and an organic, flowing shape](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/natural-wood-tactile-sensory-object-with-glass-dome-and-metal-ball-on-rustic-plank.jpg)

## Academic

At the highest level of analysis, the question of technology’s influence on our sense of community demands a critical examination of power structures, cultural norms, and the very nature of human connection in the 21st century. Sociologist Zygmunt Bauman’s concept of “liquid modernity” offers a compelling framework for understanding the fluidity and instability of social relationships in a technologically saturated world.

Bauman argued that traditional social structures and institutions are increasingly dissolving, leaving individuals feeling adrift in a sea of uncertainty. Technology, with its constant flux and rapid pace of change, accelerates this process, making it difficult to form lasting bonds and build strong communities. The “**attention economy**” further fragments our social landscape, as individuals compete for visibility and validation in online spaces.

Drawing upon the work of media theorist Marshall McLuhan, we can also consider how technology shapes our perceptions of space and time. McLuhan famously argued that the medium is the message, meaning that the technology we use to communicate influences the content of our communication. The internet, with its instant connectivity and global reach, compresses space and time, creating a sense of simultaneity that can be both exhilarating and disorienting.

This compression of space and time can weaken our ties to local communities, as we become increasingly connected to global networks. The rise of transnational corporations and globalized culture further erodes traditional forms of community identity.

![A cluster of slender, elongated objects arranged radially is depicted against a soft grey background. Some objects are off-white with subtle grey marble-like patterns, resembling natural bone or stone, and feature small integrated camera lenses or metallic tips](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/futuristic-biomorphic-tech-devices-with-coral-like-organic-accents-and-metallic-elements.jpg)

### The Algorithmic Construction of Community

A crucial aspect of technology’s influence on community lies in the algorithmic processes that shape our online experiences. Social media platforms and search engines use complex algorithms to personalize the content we see, based on our past behavior and preferences. This can create echo chambers, where we are primarily exposed to information that confirms our existing beliefs, reinforcing biases and limiting our exposure to diverse perspectives.

Furthermore, these algorithms are often opaque and unaccountable, raising concerns about manipulation and control. The Cambridge Analytica scandal, for example, revealed how personal data harvested from Facebook was used to influence political opinions and manipulate elections.

The following table illustrates the potential impact of algorithmic bias on community formation:

| Scenario | Algorithmic Bias | Impact on Community |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Online Housing Ads | Showing ads for certain neighborhoods only to specific demographic groups | Reinforces segregation and limits access to diverse communities |
| Job Recommendations | Prioritizing male candidates for STEM positions | Excludes women from opportunities and perpetuates gender inequality |
| News Feeds | Filtering out dissenting opinions and prioritizing sensational content | Polarizes communities and hinders constructive dialogue |

This table illustrates how algorithms can reinforce existing societal inequalities.

![A close-up, abstract macro image features various organic and geometric forms. In the foreground and mid-ground, numerous matte, peach-colored spherical objects are scattered among intricate, white, branching structures resembling mycelium or fungi](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/microscopic-abstract-of-organic-mycelial-structures-matte-spheres-and-textured-composite-blocks.jpg)

### The Politics of Connection

The question of technology and community is inherently political, as it involves issues of power, control, and access. Who controls the technologies that shape our social interactions? Who benefits from the data that is collected and analyzed? How can we ensure that technology is used to promote social justice and equity, rather than to exacerbate existing inequalities?

These are complex questions that require a critical and interdisciplinary approach. We need to move beyond simplistic narratives of technological progress and engage in a more nuanced analysis of the social, cultural, and political implications of technology.

To understand the full impact of technology on our sense of community, we must consider the following points:

![Square rose gold-colored modular digital display units are arranged on a light-colored, textured surface. The central unit is in sharp focus, showcasing a dark screen with a glowing, multi-colored circular icon](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/rose-gold-modular-digital-display-units-on-a-soft-textured-surface-with-pink-objects.jpg)

### Toward a Sustainable Digital Future

Building a sustainable digital future requires a fundamental shift in our relationship with technology. We need to move beyond a purely instrumental view of technology as a tool for efficiency and productivity, and embrace a more holistic and ethical approach. This involves prioritizing human well-being, social justice, and environmental [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  in the design and development of new technologies.

It also means fostering a culture of critical engagement with technology, where individuals are empowered to question, challenge, and resist the dominant narratives of technological progress. By reclaiming our agency in the digital age, we can create a [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  where technology strengthens our sense of community and promotes a more just and equitable world.

# How Has Technology Affected Social Interaction?

[By Jake Burgess](https://www.sogolytics.com/blog/author/jburgess/)

Last updated on: May 27, 2025

How Has Technology Affected Social Interaction?

by [Jake Burgess](https://www.sogolytics.com/blog/author/jburgess/) 

Estimated Reading Time :  6 mins

It’s no surprise that the world is becoming increasingly digital, but the current pace of digitization is astonishing. From AI chatbots to dating apps, tech is the new real life.

We do our shopping online, build our businesses online, spend our downtime online, and even make life-long friends online. As of 2022, 66 percent of the global population were active internet users ([Statista](https://www.statista.com/statistics/617136/digital-population-worldwide/)). Bringing us closer to commodities, the internet is set to become an even bigger part of our lives. But can technology help improve social interaction skills?

Technology is a fast-paced market, and every pioneer is in a race for first place on the podium. New innovations are piloted every day in a desperate attempt to sell what the masses will buy—tools to make our daily lives easier. As older generations warm to the idea of utilizing technology in the face of a pandemic, we move eerily closer to a world where social interaction is primarily digital.

****How is your org impacted by technology?**  
Get insights with our free [company culture climate survey template](https://www.sogolytics.com/survey-templates/employee/company-culture-survey/)!**

But should we look at this as a positive or negative change? Will human interaction strengthen or suffer as a result of new communication technologies? In this article we will explore the good, the bad, and the ugly truth of technology’s impact on social interaction.

## THE GOOD

There is no denying that the internet has had a positive impact on human communication. Compared to pre-internet times, we are now more connected than ever before. Here are some of the ways that technology has a positive effect on social interaction.

**Instant global communication**

How can technology affect social media? Most of us now have the means to contact people on the other side of the world immediately. No waiting for the postman, messenger pigeon, or message in a bottle—technology has broken down the communication barriers that distance once presented. It is a phenomenon that is commonly referred to as globalization.

**Virtual connections**

Let’s look at how technology affects human relationships. Video calling apps like Skype and Zoom mean people can now experience emotional connections without having to be in the same room. This has been especially important throughout the coronavirus crisis, where families from separate households were forced to stay apart. The day the UK lockdown was announced, [Zoom was downloaded 2.13m times](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2020/mar/31/zoom-booms-as-demand-for-video-conferencing-tech-grows-in-coronavirus-outbreak) around the world—up from 56,000 a day two months earlier.

**Online dating**

Technology has also made finding love much easier, with online dating increasing in popularity. A report released last year predicted that [more than 50% of couples will meet online by 2035](https://news.sky.com/story/finding-love-online-more-than-half-of-couples-set-to-meet-via-the-internet-11871341). The same report found 47% of people believe online dating makes it easier for introverted people to find love.

**Barrier-free interaction**

Similarly, people who would otherwise have limited social interaction now have the option to be part of online communities. People with disabilities can forget about their physical boundaries inside a video game universe, while socially anxious people can gain confidence by practicing interaction over the internet instead of face to face. Sending messages allows people the time to process information and formulate a response, whereas face to face is more immediate. On the other hand, this isn’t always a good thing.

## THE BAD

Of course, as with everything, there is another angle to consider. How does technology affect our social lives negatively? Some would argue that the more anonymous and less immediate interaction associated with digital communication is bad news. Behind the screens of smartphones and the keyboards of computers, there are also more chances for deception—particularly for vulnerable people. Here are some of the ways the technology has a negative impact on social interaction.

**Decreased human contact**

More and more people are beginning to rely on technology to communicate with their loved ones, friends and associates. The coronavirus lockdown that forced millions of people to work from home also accelerated online communication tools—meaning we get even less human contact than ever before. As of July 2020, [58% of customer interactions were digital](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/strategy-and-corporate-finance/our-insights/how-covid-19-has-pushed-companies-over-the-technology-tipping-point-and-transformed-business-forever).

As remote working becomes the norm, transactional processes are automated with self-service machines too. For many, this can theoretically be a good thing, but it also highlights how technology affects our communication negatively. The same ease of technological-driven interactions leaves some groups behind. The increase in the use of technology to communicate could also cause a rise in loneliness, especially among elderly people who may rely on these transactional encounters as their primary source of social contact.

**Are your customers satisfied with your communication? [Find out!](https://www.sogolytics.com/blog/5-key-questions-for-your-external-communication-audit/)**

**A rise in bullying**

Technology has also made cyberbullying possible, and children are now particularly vulnerable to harassment online. While cyberbullying is a worry for children, it is also becoming a common challenge among adults too. Results from a YouGov poll conducted last year showed that [a quarter of adults have experienced cyberbullying](https://www.independent.co.uk/life-style/cyberbullying-adults-online-abuse-facebook-instagram-a8891701.html). Given the disheartening rise in suicide among victims of cyberbullying, it is clear to see that technology is not always a healthy source of social interaction.

**Online deception**

Catfishing is the practice of creating a fake online identity with intent to manipulate, stalk, or abuse a specific victim. It has become a scarily common activity among dating sites and social media platforms and is the subject of a popular MTV reality TV show. A 2018 survey saw [9% respondents say that being catfished had affected their mental health](https://metro.co.uk/2018/08/06/new-survey-reveals-the-massive-toll-of-catfishing-on-victims-7764000/).

**A platform for predators**

Technology has also provided a platform for online predators to pursue their victims. And as UK schools closed during the coronavirus lockdown, children were on their devices a lot more often and faced with a sudden drop in social interaction. While there is not yet any overwhelming evidence to suggest the pandemic caused an increase in predatory activity online, The National Center for Missing & Exploited Children (NCMEC) has said [reports to their CyberTipline increased by 106%](https://www.forbes.com/sites/thomasbrewster/2020/04/24/child-exploitation-complaints-rise-106-to-hit-2-million-in-just-one-month-is-covid-19-to-blame/#24f1f2c64c9c) during the first months of the pandemic.

## The ugly truth

Whether we see it in a positive or negative light, or even a mixture of the two, the truth of the matter is that technology has had a huge impact on the way we communicate with each other. While it allows us to make instant connections with people on the other side of the world, it also puts us at [risk of loneliness](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/live-long-and-prosper/202210/technology-use-loneliness-and-isolation), as well as new forms of harassment and manipulation.

But as [new technologies](/blog/how-technology-influences-us/) are unveiled to the world, new cybersecurity regulations and other tech safety measures are likely to be put in place, too. These aren’t likely to protect everyone from the negative effects of technology, but it also rests on our own shoulders to use the internet with caution. Equally, it is our own responsibility to ensure we get enough real-life human contact in our daily lives, in addition to digital social interaction.

# How Is Digital Technology Changing Our Interactions With Each Other?

The digital revolution is greatly impacting the ways we connect and communicate.

It’s no secret that we’re living in an era where digital technology is growing at explosive rates. In fact, by 2020, 2.9 billion people are expected to be on social media.\* Couple that with person-to-person messages sent with both traditional SMS texting and messaging apps and we’re radically changing the way we interact with one another—an important revelation for anyone with a career focused on psychology. Currently, nearly 70% of Americans think all this digital technology is good for our relationships.† While we can’t be sure if that’s true, we can be certain that these newer communication technologies aren’t going away. So what does that mean for our interactions? And where do we go from here? When it comes to how we relate to one another, digital technology is:

#### Mobilizing Communities

Communities have always been central to our relationships, but digital technology is allowing people to create communities across wide reaches of geography. Social media sites like Twitter have provided a space for like-minded people to share ideas, whether it’s discussing a TV show or forming political movements. These sites have been particularly valuable to traditionally marginalized groups, playing a significant role in the creation of protest movements and support networks. One example is the LGBT community. Studies show LGBT youth have substantially more online friends than non-LGBT youth, with 50% of LGBT respondents reporting at least one close online friend, compared with only 19% of non-LGBT youth.‡ People who may have once felt isolated from others can now find and be a part of a community.

#### Keeping Relationships Going

Have you seen photos of the baby your friend from elementary school recently had? Did you get into a political debate with a cousin you haven’t seen in person in 20 years? Maybe you spent a baseball game texting with an old college buddy. If you use digital technology at all, chances are you’re staying connected with people who you might otherwise have lost touch with. In fact, a recent survey of Americans revealed that only 11% of those surveyed had actually seen any of their online friends in person over the prior 3 months.§ Digital technology has so changed the way we establish and maintain relationships that “friending” is now a regularly used verb.

#### Changing Our Love Lives

At one time, every romantic relationship began with two people meeting in person at school, or work, or at a party or bar. Now, 33% of couples have met online, and that’s expected to rise by 70% by 2040.\*\* Meanwhile, committed couples often use texting as a way to stay in touch, plan dates, or ask what the heck they were supposed to pick up from the grocery store.

#### Disrupting Politics

One of the key ways we interact with one another is through the political sphere. Debating who should represent us and what policies we should adopt are common topics of conversation between friends and strangers alike. But where most of those conversations once happened over the kitchen table or in the proverbial “smoky backroom,” they’re increasingly happening via digital technology.

The number of registered voters following politicians on social media more than doubled between 2010 and 2014,†† and that’s just the beginning of how people are using technology to communicate about politics and support politicians. Online fund-raising has allowed millions of Americans to easily donate money to causes and candidates, and the rise of blogs and independent news sites has given voice to everyone from earnest dissidents to professional propagandists. This has resulted in a political system that’s more open—and more disordered—than ever before. Whether this is a good thing or not is up for debate. But it’s unquestionable that digital technology is remaking how we interact politically.

#### Vital to Our Future

Digital technology is likely to become increasingly important to our lives. And if you’re interested in a career in psychology, it is important to understand the ways digital technology impacts lives and can change interactions. Not only can a psychology degree teach you about the forces that affect our interactions, but that knowledge can be applied in a wide range of professions, from academia to marketing.

Digital technology is dramatically changing our world. A great way to learn how this might be affecting us is by earning an [online psychology degree](/online-masters-programs/psychology). Taking Control of Your Digital Life
-----------------------------------

The Social Impact of Technology on Children
===========================================

No doubt exists that the majority of social interactions happen online these days. This is especially true during the current pandemic situation when in-person schools, recreation areas, extracurricular classes, and events are less prominent than they have been in the past. Children of all ages text each other or post on social media to chat instead of calling or meeting face-to-face. All of this technology has a considerable social impact on young children that carries through the preteen, teen, and young adult years.

**Positive Social Impact of Technology**

The main advantages of Internet-specific and tech-based communication and interaction for children and teens stem from accessibility and exposure to a diverse range of people and social situations. Before the Internet became a household thing, kids would interact with their peers at school or in extracurricular activities and neighbors in their community. These days, kids can make friends with people all around the world from diverse geographic locations, cultures, races, religions, and socioeconomic backgrounds. This exposure can boost the power of socialization in a variety of ways. It can teach tolerance and empathy and contribute to a wider understanding of the world and human behavior.

Technology also allows children who struggle with social interactions to make friends and experience many of the same things as their peers with a higher degree of comfort. This is also useful for those who may become the target of in-person bullying. That does not mean that cyberbullying is not a serious problem. The Internet and tech communication simply provide another avenue for positive experiences.

**Negative Social Impact on Children**

Cyberbullying is unfortunately quite commonplace and can cause a serious social impact to children and teens. Studies actually indicate that negative social habits get worse if children spend more time on mobile devices and the Internet. Screen time is also associated with feelings of loneliness or disconnection. The ability to connect with many different types of people can leave some feeling more left out than before.

According to a variety of studies, increased screen time and reliance on technology can lead to reduced attention spans, lower levels of privacy, which can lead to unrealistic expectations of closeness or friendship online, addictive behaviors that get in the way of schoolwork or other healthy pursuits, and general struggles to interact with people in person. As children get used to communicating in a virtually anonymous fashion with the screen as a buffer, they may become increasingly uncomfortable with direct conversation. It can also reduce their ability to understand non-verbal cues like body language and facial expressions.

Technology is here to stay, and it will continue to dominate the entertainment and socialization worlds of young people going forward. While the Internet and other tech options offer a lot of benefits, it is important to understand the social impact from a negative perspective, too. Understanding, balance, and offering other options are great ways to minimize any harmful effects that technology can bring.

### What does it mean to be digitally responsible?

We believe that it's our responsibility to use technology in a way that doesn't harm others and to be aware of the impact that technology has on our health, environment, and society at large.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital platforms enhance global connectivity, allowing users to form relationships across geographical boundaries, but they can also lead to superficial interactions and reduced face-to-face communication.
The rise of technology has shifted communication styles towards more textual and visual formats, often lacking non-verbal cues, which can lead to misunderstandings and emotional disconnect.
New trends include increased reliance on virtual interactions, online communities for niche interests, and the rise of social media as a primary communication tool, influencing relationship dynamics.
